items.question_text: Which action is prohibited in Class A airspace?

choices.text: Applying visual separation or issuing VFR or VFR-on-top clearances.
Providing IFR separation between aircraft on instrument flight plans.
Assigning headings and altitudes to IFR aircraft.
Issuing traffic advisories to IFR aircraft when needed.

items.explanation: In Class A airspace, controllers do not apply visual separation and do not issue VFR or VFR-on-top clearances.

items.question_text: When may you clear an IFR aircraft to maintain "VFR conditions"?

choices.text: When the pilot requests a VFR climb or descent, or in terminal airspace when the clearance will provide a noise-abatement benefit on a route segment that does not conform to an approved noise-abatement route or altitude.
Whenever the weather is VFR and the controller prefers to stop providing IFR separation.
Only after the pilot reports the airport in sight and accepts a visual approach.
Whenever the aircraft is operating below Class A airspace and the pilot has ATIS information.

items.explanation: The paragraph authorizes "maintain VFR conditions" only for requested VFR climb or descent situations and a specific terminal noise-abatement case.

items.question_text: If an arriving VFR pilot tells approach control "have numbers," what does that establish?

choices.text: Only that the pilot has the current landing numbers; it does not establish receipt of the ATIS broadcast.
That the pilot has received the current ATIS code and no further airport information is needed.
That the pilot is requesting sequencing service instead of tower contact instructions.
That the pilot already has all known traffic information for the airport area.

items.explanation: The note makes clear that "have numbers" does not mean the pilot has received the ATIS broadcast.

items.question_text: What kind of fix should be selected for visual holding of VFR aircraft?

choices.text: A prominent geographic fix that can be easily recognized from the air, preferably one depicted on sectional charts.
Any unpublished point the controller can describe from memory, even if it is not charted.
Only an IFR initial approach fix on a published instrument procedure.
Any location directly over the airport regardless of pilot familiarity or chart depiction.

items.explanation: Visual holding fixes should be selected from prominent geographic points that pilots can recognize easily, with depicted VFR checkpoints preferred.

items.question_text: When is visual separation specifically not authorized?

choices.text: When the lead aircraft is a super.
When the trailing aircraft is a heavy and both pilots report each other in sight.
When the weather is VFR and radar separation exists before and after the application.
When the aircraft are both below FL180 and in communication with ATC.

items.explanation: The rule expressly bars visual separation when the lead aircraft is a super, even if other conditions are favorable.

items.question_text: After an aircraft is cleared to maintain "VFR-on-top," what ATC services still continue?

choices.text: Traffic advisories, safety alerts, and merging target procedures.
None, because the pilot is solely responsible for all separation once VFR-on-top is issued.
Only wake turbulence advisories, because all radar-based services stop.
Only route amendments, because controllers may not issue safety alerts to VFR-on-top aircraft.

items.explanation: IFR separation is not applied, but controllers still provide traffic advisories, safety alerts, and merging target procedures.

items.question_text: For a VFR-on-top aircraft on a magnetic course from 180 through 359 degrees, what cruising levels apply when operating more than 3,000 feet above the surface through 18,000 feet MSL?

choices.text: Even cardinal altitudes or flight levels plus 500 feet.
Odd cardinal altitudes or flight levels plus 500 feet.
Any altitude assigned by ATC, with no directional pattern above 3,000 feet AGL.
The lowest altitude that remains clear of clouds, regardless of direction of flight.

items.explanation: The paragraph points controllers to the VFR-on-top cruising rule: 180 through 359 degrees uses even altitudes plus 500 feet.

items.question_text: What is true about a visual approach?

choices.text: It is not a standard instrument approach procedure and it has no missed approach segment.
It is a standard instrument approach that may be flown only after the pilot reports the runway environment in sight.
It includes a published missed approach segment that the pilot must fly automatically after any go-around.
It removes the need for ATC to provide IFR separation after a go-around.

items.explanation: A visual approach is an ATC authorization to proceed visually and clear of clouds; it is not a standard instrument procedure and has no missed approach segment.

items.question_text: What reported weather is required before initiating vectors for a visual approach at an airport with weather reporting?

choices.text: A ceiling at least 500 feet above the MVA or MIA and visibility of 3 miles or greater.
A ceiling at least 500 feet above airport elevation and visibility of 1 mile or greater.
Any VFR ceiling and visibility, provided the pilot accepts responsibility for terrain avoidance.
A ceiling at least 1,000 feet above the MVA or MIA and visibility of 5 miles or greater.

items.explanation: Vectors for a visual approach require the reported ceiling to be at least 500 feet above the MVA or MIA and visibility at least 3 miles.

items.question_text: When issuing a visual approach clearance at an airport without an operating control tower, how should the clearance be stated?

choices.text: Clear the aircraft for a visual approach to the airport only and do not specify a runway.
Clear the aircraft for the visual approach to the specific runway the controller expects the pilot to use.
Issue only headings and descend-via instructions until the pilot cancels IFR.
Withhold the clearance until the pilot reports both the airport and a preceding aircraft in sight.

items.explanation: At airports without an operating control tower, the visual approach clearance is issued to the airport, not to a named runway.

items.question_text: When simultaneous visual approaches are conducted to parallel runways separated by less than 2,500 feet and approved separation is not being used, what is required?

choices.text: The succeeding aircraft must report the preceding aircraft in sight on the adjacent parallel approach, and if visual separation is applied the controller must instruct it to maintain visual separation.
ATC may run the approaches without either aircraft reporting the other in sight as long as both are on final.
The lead aircraft must report the succeeding aircraft in sight and agree to maintain spacing for both aircraft.
The controller must apply 1 1/2 miles of radar separation until both aircraft land, because visual separation is never authorized to parallel runways closer than 2,500 feet.

items.explanation: For close parallel runways, the succeeding aircraft must report the preceding aircraft in sight and maintain visual separation if that method is used.

items.question_text: Before clearing an aircraft for a charted visual flight procedure when it is not following another aircraft, what must the pilot report?

choices.text: A charted visual landmark in sight.
The runway environment in sight and the localizer alive.
Only the airport beacon in sight, regardless of the published CVFP landmarks.
A preceding aircraft in sight on any runway at the airport.

items.explanation: If the aircraft is not following another aircraft, the pilot must report the charted visual landmark in sight before a CVFP can be cleared.

items.question_text: What must be assigned before issuing an RNAV visual flight procedure clearance?

choices.text: An altitude at or above the MVA or MIA.
An altitude below the MVA or MIA so the aircraft can remain clear of clouds.
A fixed altitude exactly equal to pattern altitude for the landing runway.
No altitude assignment at all, because the pilot joins the published path visually.

items.explanation: Before issuing an RVFP clearance, the aircraft must be assigned an altitude at or above the MVA or MIA.

items.question_text: When may a contact approach be cleared?

choices.text: After the pilot requests it and the other contact-approach conditions are met.
Whenever ATC believes the pilot could probably complete the flight visually, even if the pilot did not request it.
Only after the pilot has already been cleared for a visual approach to a specific runway.
Whenever ground visibility is at least 3 miles, even if no instrument approach is published for the airport.

items.explanation: ATC does not initiate a contact approach. The clearance is issued after pilot request and satisfaction of the other rule conditions.

items.question_text: Where is special VFR not authorized?

choices.text: Within Class E extension areas.
Within Class D surface areas below 10,000 feet MSL.
Within Class C surface areas when the pilot requests SVFR.
Within Class B surface areas when the weather is below basic VFR minima.

items.explanation: SVFR may be used only within the lateral boundaries of certain surface areas and is not authorized within Class E extension areas.

items.question_text: If IFR traffic prevents a special VFR clearance from being granted, what should the pilot be told?

choices.text: The anticipated delay, but no EFC or expected departure time.
An EFC time based on the next estimated IFR arrival or departure gap.
Nothing until the IFR traffic clears, because delay information should not be issued to SVFR aircraft.
A departure release time that the pilot must not exceed.

items.explanation: When IFR traffic delays SVFR, the controller tells the pilot the anticipated delay but does not issue an EFC or expected departure time.

items.question_text: What separation may be applied between a special VFR fixed-wing aircraft and an IFR aircraft?

choices.text: Nonradar or visual separation.
Radar separation only.
Pilot-applied wake turbulence separation only.
No separation is required if the fixed-wing pilot reports 1 mile flight visibility.

items.explanation: For SVFR fixed-wing operations, controllers use nonradar or visual separation. Radar separation is not authorized for those fixed-wing operations.

items.question_text: How should an altitude be assigned when using vertical separation for a special VFR aircraft?

choices.text: Clear the aircraft at or below an altitude at least 500 feet below conflicting IFR traffic, without assigning a fixed altitude.
Assign a fixed altitude exactly 500 feet below the conflicting IFR aircraft.
Assign any altitude the pilot requests as long as the aircraft stays within the surface area.
Use only radar vectors and avoid altitude assignments entirely during special VFR operations.

items.explanation: Under vertical separation for SVFR, controllers use an at-or-below altitude that stays at least 500 feet below conflicting IFR traffic and do not assign a fixed altitude.

items.question_text: When may local special VFR operations be authorized for a specified period?

choices.text: When the pilot requests them and the aircraft can be recalled if traffic or weather conditions require.
Whenever the airport is below basic VFR minima, even if the pilot did not request local operations.
Only when a written LOA already exists with the pilot operating that day.
Only for helicopters, because fixed-wing aircraft cannot be authorized for local SVFR series operations.

items.explanation: Local SVFR operations may be authorized on request if the aircraft can be recalled when traffic or weather require.

items.question_text: When may an aircraft be authorized to climb to VFR?

choices.text: When the pilot requests it and restricted visibility is the only weather limitation.
Whenever the controller prefers to move the aircraft out of the surface area quickly.
Only when the aircraft is already above the cloud tops and just needs a cruising altitude.
Whenever the reported ceiling is below 1,000 feet, regardless of pilot request.

items.explanation: Climb to VFR is authorized on pilot request when restricted visibility is the only weather limitation.

items.question_text: When official ground visibility is less than 1 mile, what allows a fixed-wing aircraft to be cleared through the surface area?

choices.text: The aircraft reports flight visibility of at least 1 statute mile.
The tower can see the aircraft from the cab and traffic is light.
The aircraft is inbound and reports the airport beacon in sight.
The pilot accepts responsibility for remaining clear of clouds, regardless of visibility.

items.explanation: When ground visibility is below 1 mile, the rule still allows clearance through the surface area if the aircraft reports flight visibility of at least 1 statute mile.

items.question_text: When weather is not reported and an arriving aircraft within the surface area says flight visibility is less than 1 mile, what should you do?

choices.text: Request the pilot's intentions.
Issue a special VFR clearance immediately if the aircraft is inbound to the primary airport.
Assign a holding fix until the pilot reports 3 miles visibility.
Issue an EFC time for the expected improvement in visibility.

items.explanation: For arriving aircraft already within the surface area and reporting less than 1 mile flight visibility, the controller requests the pilot's intentions.

items.question_text: Which service is included in basic radar service to VFR aircraft in terminal airspace?

choices.text: Limited radar vectoring when requested by the pilot.
Automatic IFR separation from all other VFR aircraft.
Mandatory holding instructions for every arrival to the primary airport.
Published instrument approach clearances on request.

items.explanation: Basic radar service includes safety alerts, traffic advisories, limited radar vectoring on request, and sequencing where procedures exist.

items.question_text: Unless the pilot states otherwise, what is assumed about arriving aircraft at the primary airport where local radar-sequencing procedures exist?

choices.text: They want radar service.
They prefer to remain nonparticipating and be sequenced only by the tower.
They want sequencing but not traffic advisories.
They will accept radar service only if ATIS says the service is mandatory.

items.explanation: For primary-airport arrivals under local procedures, aircraft are assumed to want radar service unless the pilot says otherwise.

items.question_text: What is required before a locally sighted aircraft can be positioned in the landing sequence on first radio contact?

choices.text: Coordination with approach control.
A pilot report that the traffic pattern is in sight.
An IFR cancellation from the pilot.
A radar identification by departure control.

items.explanation: A local controller who sights the aircraft at first contact may place it in sequence only after coordinating with approach control.

items.question_text: What must happen before taking action to position a VFR aircraft in the approach sequence?

choices.text: Identify the aircraft.
Issue a landing sequence number.
Assign the aircraft a fixed altitude for the entire approach.
Obtain a visual approach clearance from the pilot.

items.explanation: Before positioning the aircraft in the approach sequence, the controller must identify it.

items.question_text: Where should VFR aircraft be held when holding is required to establish an approach sequence?

choices.text: Over the initial reporting fix or a fix near the airport.
Only over the departure end of the active runway.
On the final approach course outside the FAF.
Only at published IFR holding fixes.

items.explanation: The holding point for these VFR aircraft is the initial reporting fix or another fix near the airport.

items.question_text: When may approach control assign landing sequence numbers while establishing aircraft in the approach sequence?

choices.text: Only when that responsibility has been delegated in a LOA or facility directive.
Whenever the pilot asks for sequence information.
Whenever the radar controller has the airport in sight.
Only when traffic is IFR rather than VFR.

items.explanation: Landing sequence numbers are normally a tower function unless delegated by LOA or facility directive.

items.question_text: What must be established before instructing a VFR aircraft to enter the traffic pattern at a specified point or vectoring it into the approach sequence?

choices.text: Radar contact.
A pilot report of the airport in sight.
A landing clearance from the tower.
A complete readback of the pattern entry instructions.

items.explanation: The controller establishes radar contact before giving specified pattern-entry or sequencing vectors to a VFR aircraft.

items.question_text: When may a VFR aircraft be told to contact the tower before the tower has been advised of the aircraft's position?

choices.text: When the tower says the aircraft is in sight and space is available in the landing sequence.
Whenever the aircraft is within 5 miles of the runway, regardless of tower awareness.
Only after the pilot reports the runway in sight and accepts responsibility for sequencing.
Only when STARS track data is not displayed in the tower.

items.explanation: Prior tower position advice may be omitted only when the tower already has the aircraft in sight and space exists in the sequence.

items.question_text: What should happen if an aircraft under tower control abandons the approach and there is no immediate space in the approach sequence?

choices.text: Instruct the aircraft to change to approach control for sequencing.
Keep the aircraft on tower frequency and have it orbit over the runway threshold.
Issue a landing clearance for any runway the pilot prefers once traffic permits.
Tell the pilot to cancel radar service and proceed VFR at pilot discretion.

items.explanation: If the aircraft abandons the approach and the sequence cannot absorb it immediately, tower sends it back to approach for resequencing.

items.question_text: When may traffic advisories be provided to a departing VFR aircraft that requested them?

choices.text: After the departure has been radar identified.
Immediately on takeoff roll if the pilot requested them on ground control.
Only after the aircraft leaves the Class D surface area.
Only after the aircraft establishes two-way communications with ARTCC.

items.explanation: The departure is told when and where to call departure control, and traffic advisories are provided after radar identification.

items.question_text: When should radar service be terminated for aircraft landing at airports where sequencing service is not provided?

choices.text: At a sufficient distance from the airport to let the pilot change to the proper frequency for traffic and airport information.
As soon as the aircraft reports the airport in sight.
Only after the aircraft lands and clears the runway.
When the aircraft is 1 mile from the runway regardless of airport complexity.

items.explanation: Radar service terminates far enough out to allow the pilot to switch to the right frequency and get local traffic or airport information.

items.question_text: What should not be assigned when the tower is not in operation?

choices.text: A landing sequence.
Traffic information.
Wind direction and velocity when available.
Notice that radar service is terminated.

items.explanation: When the tower is inoperative, wind, traffic information, and radar-termination information are provided, but landing sequence is not assigned.

items.question_text: What do TRSA procedures add to VFR terminal operations?

choices.text: They apply within the TRSA in addition to the basic radar services already described for VFR terminal aircraft.
They replace all basic radar services with IFR-only procedures.
They apply only to nonparticipating aircraft that decline radar service.
They apply only to departures and not to arrivals operating in the TRSA.

items.explanation: TRSA procedures are layered on top of the basic radar services already provided to VFR aircraft in terminal airspace.

items.question_text: What must you tell a VFR aircraft that is being held inside or outside the TRSA?

choices.text: When to expect further clearance.
Its assigned landing runway and parking gate.
That radar service is automatically terminated.
That it must cancel TRSA participation before rejoining the sequence.

items.explanation: The paragraph requires controllers to provide an expect-further-clearance time when VFR aircraft are held in relation to the TRSA.

items.question_text: Which is an approved separation method between participating VFR aircraft and IFR or other participating VFR aircraft in a TRSA?

choices.text: Visual separation, 500 feet vertical separation, or target resolution except when ISR is displayed.
Pilot self-separation only, because TRSA participation is voluntary.
Radar vectors only, because visual separation is not used in a TRSA.
A minimum of 3 miles radar separation in every case.

items.explanation: The paragraph allows separation by visual separation, 500 feet vertical separation, or target resolution, with the ISR limitation noted.

items.question_text: Even though separation is not required between helicopters or powered-lift aircraft in a TRSA, what must still be provided as needed?

choices.text: Traffic information.
A full IFR clearance.
A special VFR clearance.
A runway assignment from approach control.

items.explanation: Helicopters and powered-lift aircraft need not be separated from each other in this paragraph, but traffic information is still exchanged as necessary.

items.question_text: What must you tell a VFR aircraft that was assigned an altitude contrary to 14 CFR 91.159 when that assignment is no longer needed or the aircraft is leaving the TRSA?

choices.text: Resume appropriate VFR altitudes.
Maintain the last assigned altitude until landing unless the pilot requests a change.
Squawk VFR and descend at pilot discretion without any further altitude information.
Return to the minimum IFR altitude for the route segment.

items.explanation: When the nonstandard VFR altitude is no longer required for separation, the aircraft must be told to resume appropriate VFR altitudes.

items.question_text: Who specifies the approach interval in a TRSA?

choices.text: The tower.
The center controlling the overlying airspace.
The pilot requesting TRSA service.
Whichever facility issued the squawk code.

items.explanation: The tower specifies the approach interval for TRSA operations.

items.question_text: If a departing aircraft within the TRSA says nothing about service preference, what is assumed?

choices.text: The aircraft wants TRSA service.
The aircraft wants only a squawk code but no departure control contact.
The aircraft wants to remain outside TRSA procedures unless it later requests them airborne.
The aircraft wants traffic advisories only after leaving the TRSA.

items.explanation: Departing aircraft in the TRSA are assumed to want TRSA service unless they say negative TRSA service or similar.

items.question_text: What are Class C services designed to do?

choices.text: Keep ATC informed of all aircraft within Class C airspace, not exclude operations.
Exclude all VFR traffic unless the airport is operating IFR only.
Provide mandatory IFR separation to every aircraft in the outer area, regardless of participation.
Replace all local procedures with TRSA procedures whenever a transponder is inoperative.

items.explanation: Class C services are intended to keep ATC informed about aircraft in the airspace, not to shut aircraft out of it.

items.question_text: If holding becomes necessary in Class C service, what must the controller do?

choices.text: Inform the pilot of the expected length of delay.
Terminate Class C service immediately and direct the aircraft to remain clear of the area.
Assign a landing sequence number and leave the pilot on tower frequency.
Hold only IFR aircraft and never VFR aircraft in the Class C area.

items.explanation: Aircraft should not normally be held in Class C, but if holding is necessary the pilot must be told the expected delay.

items.question_text: How may VFR aircraft be separated from IFR aircraft in Class C airspace?

choices.text: By visual separation, 500 feet vertical separation, or target resolution except when ISR is displayed.
Only by 3 miles radar separation because visual separation is not used in Class C.
Only by pilot self-separation after traffic advisories are issued.
Only by altitude assignments consistent with 14 CFR 91.159.

items.explanation: The Class C separation methods match the paragraph: visual separation, 500 feet vertical, or target resolution subject to the ISR limitation.

items.question_text: What does a response of "(call sign) standby" mean to a pilot approaching Class C airspace?

choices.text: Two-way radio communications have been established and the pilot may enter the Class C airspace.
The pilot must remain outside the Class C airspace until the controller issues the word "approved."
The pilot may enter only the outer area but not the Class C core.
The pilot must squawk standby and await a transponder code before entry.

items.explanation: A reply using the aircraft call sign and "standby" establishes two-way communications for Class C entry.

items.question_text: When must a VFR aircraft assigned an altitude contrary to 14 CFR 91.159 be told to resume appropriate VFR altitudes in Class C service?

choices.text: When the altitude is no longer needed for separation, when leaving the outer area, or when Class C service is terminated.
Only after the pilot requests a cruise altitude change.
Only after the aircraft lands at the primary airport.
Only if the aircraft was also assigned a radar heading.

items.explanation: The paragraph identifies three trigger points for the resume-altitudes instruction: no longer needed for separation, leaving the outer area, or termination of Class C service.

items.question_text: Which aircraft receive a Class C exception from IFR separation under this rule?

choices.text: Hot air balloons need not be separated from IFR aircraft, and VFR helicopters or powered-lift aircraft need not be separated from IFR helicopters or powered-lift aircraft.
Any VFR aircraft operating below 1,000 feet AGL is excepted from IFR separation in Class C.
Only gliders are excepted from IFR separation in the Class C area.
All helicopters are excepted from separation with all IFR aircraft, regardless of type.

items.explanation: The exceptions paragraph names hot air balloons and same-category helicopter or powered-lift pairings, while still requiring traffic information and safety alerts as appropriate.

items.question_text: How are departures from uncontrolled airports within Class C airspace handled?

choices.text: Using procedures advertised in a Letter to Airmen.
Exactly like departures from the primary airport, with no special local publication needed.
Only after individual verbal coordination with every adjacent tower on each flight.
By denying departure until the aircraft can be issued an IFR clearance.

items.explanation: For uncontrolled airports within Class C, the handling procedures are published in a Letter to Airmen.

choices.text: Change to advisory frequency approved, N123AB.
Change to approach frequency approved, N123AB.
Remain on this frequency until touchdown, N123AB.
Cleared to leave Charlie service on your discretion, N123AB.

items.explanation: One approved termination phrase here is CHANGE TO ADVISORY FREQUENCY APPROVED.

items.situation: A VFR aircraft receiving Class C service is landing at a secondary airport in the Class C outer area. Another controller wants to keep the aircraft on frequency until touchdown even though the pilot still needs time to switch over and obtain the local traffic and airport information.
items.para_context: For aircraft landing at airports other than the primary airport, terminate service far enough out to let the pilot change to the proper frequency for traffic and airport information.

choices.text: Terminate the service at a sufficient distance to let the pilot change to the appropriate advisory or airport-information frequency before landing at the secondary airport.
Keep the aircraft on Class C service until touchdown because secondary-airport arrivals must remain on approach frequency for the entire landing.
Terminate service immediately without regard to frequency-change timing because Class C service ends as soon as an aircraft announces a landing airport.
Transfer the aircraft to the primary airport tower frequency because all Class C terminations must use the primary-airport frequency first.

items.explanation: Aircraft landing at other than the primary airport are terminated at a sufficient distance to allow the proper frequency change for local traffic and airport information.

items.question_text: When may Class C service end before the aircraft leaves the associated outer area?

choices.text: When the aircraft is landing at a secondary airport or has requested termination of service while in the outer area.
Whenever the aircraft changes from IFR to VFR while still inbound to the primary airport.
Whenever the pilot reports the airport in sight, regardless of destination airport.
Only after the tower approves termination on the local control frequency.

items.explanation: Otherwise, services continue until the aircraft departs the outer area; the early-end cases are landings at secondary airports or pilot-requested termination in the outer area.

items.question_text: What equipment is generally required to operate an aircraft within Class B airspace?

choices.text: An operable two-way radio and the applicable operating transponder with automatic altitude reporting equipment.
Only a two-way radio, because Class B does not require altitude reporting once ATC radar contact is established.
Only an operating transponder, because voice communication is optional for VFR aircraft in Class B.
No special equipment if the aircraft remains below the Class B floor while inside the lateral limits.

items.explanation: The paragraph points to the basic Class B equipment requirement: two-way radio plus the appropriate transponder and altitude-reporting equipment.

items.question_text: What must a VFR aircraft obtain before operating in Class B airspace?

choices.text: An ATC clearance.
Only two-way communications with the Class B facility.
A traffic advisory and a squawk code, but not a clearance.
Only a tower-issued takeoff clearance if departing from a controlled airport.

items.explanation: VFR aircraft need a Class B clearance before operating in the Class B airspace.

items.question_text: To the extent practical, how should large turbine engine-powered airplanes be cleared to and from the primary airport in Class B airspace?

choices.text: Using routes and altitudes that avoid VFR corridors and the airspace below the Class B floor where VFR aircraft are operating.
Using the lowest practical altitudes beneath the Class B floor so the Class B core remains open to VFR traffic.
By releasing them into any VFR corridor so long as wake turbulence advisories are issued.
By keeping them outside Class B airspace until the aircraft is established on final.

items.explanation: The methods paragraph aims to keep large turbine traffic away from VFR corridors and lower VFR operating areas whenever practical.

items.question_text: What minimum separation is required between a VFR aircraft and a VFR or IFR aircraft weighing more than 19,000 pounds or a turbojet in Class B airspace?

choices.text: At least 1 1/2 miles, 500 feet vertical separation, or visual separation.
At least 3 miles or 1,000 feet in every case.
Only target resolution, because Class B service does not use visual separation.
No specific minimum if both aircraft are VFR and radar identified.

items.explanation: For aircraft above 19,000 pounds and turbojets, the Class B rule uses 1 1/2 miles, 500 feet vertical, or visual separation.

items.question_text: What is mandatory between all aircraft receiving Class B service?

choices.text: Traffic advisories and safety alerts, with merging target procedures applied as required.
Visual separation from every other aircraft before entry into Class B airspace.
A runway assignment and landing sequence number from approach control.
Target resolution from every aircraft, including helicopters and balloons.

items.explanation: The traffic-advisories paragraph requires mandatory traffic advisories and safety alerts between all aircraft and points controllers to the merging target rule.

items.situation: Two aircraft under Class B service are converging and their targets are beginning to merge. Another controller says no advisory is necessary because both pilots are VFR and can sort it out visually.
items.para_context: In Class B service, traffic advisories and safety alerts are mandatory between all aircraft, and merging target procedures still apply.

choices.text: Issue the required traffic advisory or safety alert as appropriate and apply merging target procedures.
Withhold the advisory because Class B traffic advisories are optional when both aircraft are VFR.
Terminate Class B service to both aircraft so the merging target rule no longer applies.
Wait until one pilot reports the other in sight before deciding whether an advisory is necessary.

items.explanation: Class B service does not make traffic advisories optional just because both aircraft are VFR. Mandatory traffic advisories, safety alerts, and merging target procedures still apply.

items.question_text: Even though separation is not required between certain helicopter or powered-lift pairings in Class B service, what must still be issued as appropriate?

choices.text: Traffic advisories and safety alerts.
Special VFR clearances.
Visual approach clearances to the nearest landing area.
Standard IFR missed-approach instructions.

items.explanation: The paragraph exempts certain helicopter and powered-lift combinations from separation, but not from traffic advisories and safety alerts.

items.question_text: What must you tell a VFR aircraft that was assigned an altitude contrary to 14 CFR 91.159 when that altitude is no longer required or the aircraft is leaving Class B airspace?

choices.text: Resume appropriate VFR altitudes.
Maintain the assigned altitude until clear of all terminal airspace.
Descend at pilot discretion to remain below the Bravo floor.
Contact the tower for a new altitude assignment before leaving the airspace.

items.explanation: The controller must tell the aircraft to resume the normal VFR altitude pattern once the contrary assignment is no longer required or the aircraft leaves Class B.

items.question_text: Who specifies the approach interval in the Class B service area?

choices.text: The tower.
The overlying ARTCC.
Departure control.
The pilot following the arrival sequence.

items.explanation: As with the TRSA provision, the Class B paragraph assigns the approach interval function to the tower.
